items.q: What is the difference between a natural hazard and a natural disaster?
items.correct: A natural hazard is a physical event with potential to cause harm; it becomes a disaster only when it affects vulnerable people and causes significant impact.
items.misconception: They are the same thing — any earthquake or hurricane is a disaster.
items.random_wrong: A hazard happens on land and a disaster happens at sea.
items.why: Earthquakes in unpopulated areas are hazards but not disasters. The 'disaster' status depends on human vulnerability — central to modern hazard geography.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.
The answer examiners want is: 7.0. “8.0” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Risk equation includes which factor in numerator?
items.why: It's Vulnerability. “Capacity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 220. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
